focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

By four-thirty the heat had not left the garage apartment. It sat in the block walls with the bleach sting from yesterday's mop water and the old frying oil that never quite washed out of the curtains. Delroy dressed in the dark until the shoulder made that impossible. Then he clicked on the stove light and worked his right arm into a clean shirt one inch at a time, jaw locked, the cuff brushing the enamel sink because he could not lift higher without the whole side of him going hot and mean.

On the table, under the edge of the rice tin so Micah would not knock it down, lay Tiana's document folder. It was blue plastic, split at one corner, tabbed with index-card strips cut so straight they looked measured. Inside were a birth certificate copy, school letters, and aid printouts. The stack had the neat, desperate look of something that had already been asked to prove too much. Delroy kept his eyes on it long enough for the pain in his shoulder to get mixed up with the other thing, the older thing, the one that had not stopped working on him since the night he admitted where her document money had gone and tore the house open. He had taken money from that future and turned it into rent, light, groceries, bus fare, into days bought one at a time. It had been necessary at the time and shameful after. Both truths could sit in the same chair and stare at him.

Marcia spoke before he heard her move. "You leaving now?"

Her voice came from the bed, flat with tiredness rather than sleep. She had not been resting anyway. Celia had been dead less than two days. Grief and money had crowded the room so completely there was no good corner left to lie down in.

"Claude said five o'clock pickup," Delroy said.

Marcia turned over. In the stove light her face looked smaller...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...barber shops all waiting for their signs to mean something again. The city never really slept. It just shifted from one set of laboring bodies to another.

Her phone vibrated once.

`You good?`

She typed `On the way` and sent it before she could turn the answer into something cleaner.

When she reached the side gate, the yard looked indecently ordinary. A pair of damp towels hung over the line behind the main house. Somebody had put out trash. The broken back light was still broken. Tiana stood there a moment with her hand on the latch, the whole night pressed into her skin under yesterday's dress.

She lifted the gate with both hands and eased it shut behind her so the rust would not complain.

Inside, the apartment smelled like coffee boiled too long, sweat gone stale, and the first heat coming back. Marcia sat at the table in one of her house dresses with a scarf tied back from her face. The partner notebook was gone. In its place sat a legal pad covered in due dates and crossed-out numbers. Micah was asleep under the fan with his phone loose in his hand and one foot kicked clear of the sheet. Delroy lay on the bed against the wall under the thin cover, eyes shut with the worked stillness of somebody pretending sleep could count as absence. His boots were on the floor beside him. He had not gone anywhere.

Marcia looked up. Her gaze moved over Tiana's face, her hair, the dress she had worn yesterday and again this morning. Tired recognition settled there, along with a question she did not spend.

Nothing had been solved. The card was still expired. UF was still on hold. Rent would still come due whether people had lied or fucked or prayed or not. The house had only taken on one more secret, and this one was still on her skin.

Tiana set her bag down without a word.
